column1: What is the HAL?;The Hardware Abstraction Layer — high-level and portable. You work with handle structures (e.g. UART_HandleTypeDef) and call functions like HAL_UART_Transmit(&huart2
column4: timeout). It hides register details and ports easily across STM32 chips
column7: and its timing is hard to predict.

column1: What is the LL library?;The Low-Layer library — thin
column2: mostly static inline wrappers mapping almost one-to-one onto register operations (e.g. LL_USART_TransmitData8). Tiny
column4: but closer to the metal and less portable.

column1: When do you use HAL versus LL?;HAL to bring a system up quickly and for code that is not timing-critical. Drop to LL (or raw registers) on the hot paths — inside ISRs
column3: or a flash-starved chip — where you need speed
column5: or size. They can coexist in one project.

column1: How does HAL handle an interrupt (three-layer dispatch)?;The vector entry USART2_IRQHandler calls the generic HAL_UART_IRQHandler(&huart2)
column6: and then calls a callback you override (e.g. HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback). You never touch a status bit.

column1: How does LL handle an interrupt?;There is no dispatch layer — the IRQ handler is your code
column2: checking and clearing flags directly (e.g. if LL_USART_IsActiveFlag_RXNE then read the data). Fewer cycles and full control
column3: but you are responsible for every flag.

column1: What is a callback?;A function you write that the library calls when an event occurs — inversion of control. The library exposes a named hook (e.g. HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback) and promises to call it. You provide the body

What is a weak symbol?;A symbol marked __attribute__((weak)) provides a default definition the linker uses only if no other (strong) definition exists. A same-named function without weak silently overrides it — no error
How do weak symbols make HAL callbacks work?;The HAL ships an empty weak default for every callback
column2: so the link always succeeds. If you define your own callback with the same name
column3: yours (strong) is linked and the empty weak one is dropped — you override exactly the callbacks you care about.

column1: How do weak aliases power the vector table?;Every startup vector entry is a weak alias to a single Default_Handler (an infinite loop). An unused IRQ still points somewhere valid
column2: and the instant you define a strong handler with the exact name
column3: the vector points at your code. That is why naming a function correctly installs an ISR.

column1: Why might my callback or ISR never run despite compiling?;Almost always a name/signature mismatch. A typo (HAL_UART_RxCallback instead of RxCpltCallback
column2: or Timer2_IRQHandler instead of TIM2_IRQHandler) creates an unrelated function
column3: leaves the weak default in place
column4: and your handler is silently never called. The weak mechanism matches by exact name.

column1: Why might adding a second USART2_IRQHandler cause multiple definition even with weak symbols?;CubeMX emits a strong (non-weak) handler
column2: and two strong definitions of the same symbol is a multiple-definition error. Weak overriding only works when exactly one definition is weak — edit the generated handler (in its USER CODE markers) or remove it so only your strong one remains.
